The **2010 NFL season** was the 91st [regular season](/wiki/Regular_season_(NFL) "Regular season (NFL)") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 45th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era.

The regular season began with the [NFL Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") on Thursday, September 9, at the [Louisiana Superdome](/wiki/Louisiana_Superdome "Louisiana Superdome") as the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2010_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2010 New Orleans Saints season"), the [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") champions, defeated the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2010_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2010 Minnesota Vikings season").

[Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), quarterback of the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2010_New_England_Patriots_season "2010 New England Patriots season"), was named [NFL MVP](/wiki/National_Football_League_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "National Football League Most Valuable Player Award") for the 2010 season. In [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV"), the league's championship game played at [Cowboys Stadium](/wiki/Cowboys_Stadium "Cowboys Stadium") in [Arlington, Texas](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas"), the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2010_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2010 Green Bay Packers season") defeated the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2010_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2010 Pittsburgh Steelers season") 31–25 to win their fourth [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), spoiling the Steelers' chance for a seventh title.[[2]](#cite_note-Super_Bowl_date-2) This season also marked the first full-length season in which a team with a losing record made the playoffs, when the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2010_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2010 Seattle Seahawks season") won the NFC West with a 7–9 record. One week later, the Seahawks dethroned the defending champion New Orleans Saints in the wild-card round, to become the first ever sub-.500 playoff team to win a postseason game.

## Labor issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Labor issues")]

NFL owners voted in 2008 to opt out of their [collective bargaining agreement](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") (CBA) with the [National Football League Players Association](/wiki/National_Football_League_Players_Association "National Football League Players Association") (NFLPA) as of the end of the 2010 season. (The vote was 23 in favor, 9 against; the extension measure needed 24 to pass, which would have set the CBA to expire after the 2012 season). Since a new CBA was not reached with the NFLPA, 2010 was an uncapped season,[[4]](#cite_note-4) meaning that there was no [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") or salary floor between which teams had to operate.[[5]](#cite_note-Uncapped-5) Also, the uncapped season limited unrestricted free agency only to players with at least six years of experience, as opposed to four under a capped season.[[5]](#cite_note-Uncapped-5) The final eight teams alive in the [2009–10 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NFL_playoffs "2009–10 NFL playoffs") (Arizona, Dallas, Minnesota and New Orleans in the NFC; and Baltimore, Indianapolis, the New York Jets and San Diego in the AFC) were restricted in the free agents they could sign.[[5]](#cite_note-Uncapped-5)

The issue of a CBA continued into the [2011 NFL season](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season"), affecting most of the off-season.

Further information: [2011 NFL lockout](/wiki/2011_NFL_lockout "2011 NFL lockout")

## Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Player movement")]

Free agency began on March 5, 2010.

### Free agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Free agency")]

Notable players to change teams during free agency included:

* Quarterbacks [Derek Anderson](/wiki/Derek_Anderson_(American_football) "Derek Anderson (American football)") (Cleveland to Arizona), [David Carr](/wiki/David_Carr_(American_football) "David Carr (American football)") (N.Y. Giants to San Francisco), [Jake Delhomme](/wiki/Jake_Delhomme "Jake Delhomme") (Carolina to Cleveland) and [Matt Leinart](/wiki/Matt_Leinart "Matt Leinart") (Arizona to Houston).
* Running backs [Thomas Jones](/wiki/Thomas_Jones_(American_football) "Thomas Jones (American football)") (N.Y. Jets to Kansas City), [Chester Taylor](/wiki/Chester_Taylor "Chester Taylor") (Minnesota to Chicago), [LaDainian Tomlinson](/wiki/LaDainian_Tomlinson "LaDainian Tomlinson") (San Diego to N.Y. Jets) and [Brian Westbrook](/wiki/Brian_Westbrook "Brian Westbrook") (Philadelphia to San Francisco).

## Draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Draft")]

Main article: [2010 NFL draft](/wiki/2010_NFL_draft "2010 NFL draft")

The league's 75th annual selection meeting, more commonly known as the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft"), took place at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") in New York City from April 22–24, the first time that the draft was held over three days instead of the usual two.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In the draft with the first overall pick, the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") chose quarterback [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") from the [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma").

## Officiating changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Officiating changes")]

[Mike Pereira](/wiki/Mike_Pereira "Mike Pereira") resigned as the league's Vice President of Officiating. He had led the NFL's officiating since 2001. Carl Johnson was named as Pereira's successor.

[Clete Blakeman](/wiki/Clete_Blakeman "Clete Blakeman") was promoted to referee, and [Don Carey](/wiki/Don_Carey_(American_football_official) "Don Carey (American football official)") returned to his back judge position.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following rule changes were passed at the league's annual owners meeting in March:

* The [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") procedure for postseason games was changed. Instead of a straight [sudden death](/wiki/Sudden_death_(sport) "Sudden death (sport)") period in which the first team to score by any method wins, the game will not immediately end if the team that receives the ball first scores a field goal on its first possession (the game will still end if they score a touchdown or if the defense scores a touchdown or safety).[[36]](#cite_note-ESPN_5022064-36) Instead, the other team gets a possession. If the second team on offense then scores a touchdown, it is declared the winner. If the score is tied after both teams had a possession, the rest of the overtime is played under sudden death. These changes were passed in response to recent statistics which showed that since 1994, teams that won the overtime period coin toss won 59.8 percent of the time, and won 34.4 percent of the time on the first possession on a field goal.[[36]](#cite_note-ESPN_5022064-36) In May, the league decided against applying these overtime rule changes to regular season games as well,[[37]](#cite_note-37) although this was adopted [two years later](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season").
* The definition of a "defenseless receiver" (in which a receiver cannot be hit in the head or neck area by an opponent who launches himself and makes contact with his helmet, shoulder, or forearm) will now apply to every defenseless player.[[38]](#cite_note-Philly89095297-38)
* A play will now immediately be whistled [dead](/wiki/Dead_ball "Dead ball") if a ball carrier's helmet is removed.[[38]](#cite_note-Philly89095297-38)
* The position of the [umpire](/wiki/Official_(American_football) "Official (American football)") has been moved from behind the defensive linebackers (except in the last two minutes of the first half, the last five minutes of the second half/overtime, and anytime the offense is inside the defense's five-yard-line) to the offensive backfield opposite the throwing arm of the quarterback in order to reduce the numerous times that the official has been run over during plays.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)
* During field goal and extra point attempts, defenders cannot line up directly across from the [long snapper](/wiki/Long_snapper "Long snapper").[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)
* Dead ball 15-yard personal fouls that are committed on the final play of either the second or fourth quarters will be assessed on the second half or overtime kickoff, respectively. Previously, such penalties during those situations were not enforced.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)
* Punt returners who make a [fair catch](/wiki/Fair_catch "Fair catch") signal but then muff the ball are entitled the opportunity to catch the ball before it hits the ground without interference. If there is interference during such a scenario, the receiving team is awarded the ball at the spot of the foul, but no penalty yardage is assessed.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)
* The 2009 temporary modification[[40]](#cite_note-ruleDALStadium-40) to the rules regarding balls in play that strike an object such as a video board or a guide wire has been made permanent.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39) Prior to 2009, only the down was replayed. The 2009 modification added resetting the game clock to the time when the original play was snapped.[[40]](#cite_note-ruleDALStadium-40)
* The replay system will now also be allowed to cover whether there was some sort of interference with the ball during a play.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)
* If the clock is stopped in the final minute of either half for a replay review, but would not have stopped without the review, there will be a 10-second runoff that neither team is allowed to decline (similar to when the offensive team commits a penalty inside of one minute in order to preserve time). As with any other 10-second runoff, either team may take a time-out in lieu of the runoff.[[39]](#cite_note-ESPN_5024325-39)

### Crowd noise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Crowd noise")]

The NFL relaxed all rules regarding crowd noise, citing the need to increase the in-stadium experience to lure more fans to attend games. In addition, the league cited the advances in the coach-to-quarterback radio communications, and more visiting teams using silent snap counts as an alternative to overcome crowd noise.[[41]](#cite_note-USATodayNoise-41)

The NFL's rules to "legislate the fans", and help visiting offensive players hear the snap count, have been controversial from the start. In one notorious example, then-[Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/Cincinnati_Bengals "Cincinnati Bengals") head coach [Sam Wyche](/wiki/Sam_Wyche "Sam Wyche") and then-quarterback [Boomer Esiason](/wiki/Boomer_Esiason "Boomer Esiason") "protested" the crowd noise rules during a 1989 nationally televised preseason game against the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") by constantly complaining to the referee about the loud crowd noise inside the [Superdome](/wiki/Louisiana_Superdome "Louisiana Superdome").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The league will still allow stadiums to post visual noise meters and other scoreboard messages to incite fans to make noise, but they must cease when the play clock is down to 15 seconds. However, home teams are still prohibited from pumping in [artificial crowd noise](/wiki/Artificial_crowd_noise "Artificial crowd noise").[[41]](#cite_note-USATodayNoise-41)

### Crackdown on illegal hits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Crackdown on illegal hits")]

After several violent hits throughout the NFL made the news in Week 3, the league announced that it would consider suspending players for illegal hits, such as helmet-to-helmet hits or other blows to the head.[[43]](#cite_note-NYTimes_Crackdown-43) (Previously, players could only be fined for such hits.) The league also instructed all officials and referees to have an even higher level of attention toward flagrant hits.[[44]](#cite_note-Yahoo_Crackdown-44) Game officials were also instructed to err on the side of safety, and throw flags even when in doubt.[[45]](#cite_note-NFLcom_Crackdown-45)

The crackdown has been controversial. Many defensive players have complained that the league is being too strict in their interpretation of what constitutes an "illegal hit", and that it forces them to behave significantly differently from how they were taught to play the game.[[43]](#cite_note-NYTimes_Crackdown-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Yahoo_Crackdown-44) Another concern is the league's instructions to game officials to err on the side of caution, since questionable calls late in close games significantly affect their outcome. However, the medical community has supported the move, believing that it will help reduce concussions and other head injuries.[[44]](#cite_note-Yahoo_Crackdown-44)

The league did not end up suspending any players for violent or illegal hits, however several players were fined for these types of hits within the first few weeks of the crackdown.

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Regular season")]

The 2010 regular season was the first year that the league used a modified version of the scheduling formula that was first introduced in [2002](/wiki/2002_NFL_season "2002 NFL season"), in which all teams play each other at least once every four years, and play in every other team's stadium at least once every eight years (notwithstanding the regular season games played overseas as part of the [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series")). Under the original 2002 formula, since the pairings were strictly based on [alphabetical order](/wiki/Alphabetical_order "Alphabetical order"), those teams scheduled to play the entire [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") had to travel to both [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") in the same season, while those teams playing the entire [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") had to make their way to both [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") and [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks").[[58]](#cite_note-2010_formula-58) In [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season"), [New England](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") and the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") each had to make cross-country trips to *all four* of the aforementioned West Coast teams. In an effort to relieve east coast teams from having to travel to the West Coast multiple times during the same season, teams will only have to visit one West Coast team ([AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") or [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")), plus one western team from the same division closer to the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), under the 2010 modified formula. Specifically, those teams traveling to Oakland (Las Vegas since [2020](/wiki/2020_NFL_season "2020 NFL season")) will also play at [Denver](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos"), while those playing at San Diego (Los Angeles Chargers area since [2017](/wiki/2017_NFL_season "2017 NFL season")) will also play at [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs"). For teams scheduled to play the NFC West, those traveling to San Francisco will also go to [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"), while those scheduled to play in Seattle will then go to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") (this became moot in [2016](/wiki/2016_NFL_season "2016 NFL season") when the Rams returned to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in the Rams area).[[58]](#cite_note-2010_formula-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

For the 2010 season, the intraconference and interconference matchups are:

**Intraconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North")
* [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South")
* [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")
* [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")

**Interconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")
* [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")
* [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")
* [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")

The entire 2010 regular-season schedule was unveiled at 7:00 pm EDT on Tuesday, April 20. Additionally, schedule release shows aired on both the NFL Network and as a [SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter") special on [ESPN2](/wiki/ESPN2 "ESPN2").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Opening weekend

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Opening weekend")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg/250px-NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg)

[St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") against home to [Carolina](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") in Week 8 of the season, on October 31, 2010

The [NFL Kickoff Game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"), the first game of the season, took place on Thursday, September 9, starting at 8:35 pm [EDT](/wiki/North_American_Eastern_Time_Zone "North American Eastern Time Zone"), with the [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") champion [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2010_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2010 New Orleans Saints season") hosting the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2010_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2010 Minnesota Vikings season"), in a rematch of the [2009](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season") [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game"), with New Orleans winning. Like in previous years, the opening week's prime-time games were expected to be announced at the NFL's annual owners meetings in late March, but that wasn't the case this year, with the schedule announced on April 20.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

On March 15, the NFL announced that both the [New York Giants](/wiki/2010_New_York_Giants_season "2010 New York Giants season") and [New York Jets](/wiki/2010_New_York_Jets_season "2010 New York Jets season") would play at home during Week 1 to open [New Meadowlands Stadium](/wiki/Metlife_Stadium "Metlife Stadium").[[63]](#cite_note-63) The Giants played on Sunday afternoon against the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2010_Carolina_Panthers_season "2010 Carolina Panthers season") and the Jets opened ESPN's *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* schedule against the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") the next night. For the nightcap, the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2010_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2010 San Diego Chargers season") traveled to play their [division rivals](/wiki/Chargers%E2%80%93Chiefs_rivalry "Chargers–Chiefs rivalry") the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2010_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2010 Kansas City Chiefs season"), marking the first time that a team from outside the [Mountain](/wiki/Mountain_Time_Zone "Mountain Time Zone") or [Pacific Time Zones](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone") has played in, or hosted, the "late" (10:15 pm ET) game.

### International play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: International play")]

See also: [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") and [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series")

The 2010 season featured one International game, played at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in London.[[64]](#cite_note-bbc1-64) The teams for this game were confirmed on January 15, 2010, with the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2010_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2010 San Francisco 49ers season") playing host to the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2010_Denver_Broncos_season "2010 Denver Broncos season") on October 31, at 1:00 pm EDT (5:00 pm [GMT](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time")),[[65]](#cite_note-65) with San Francisco winning.

The following week, the third regular-season game of the [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series") featured the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2010_Buffalo_Bills_season "2010 Buffalo Bills season") hosting the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2010_Chicago_Bears_season "2010 Chicago Bears season") at [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")'s [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre") on November 7 at 1 pm EST, marking the first time that the regular-season portion of the series has taken place during the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") season and the first time an NFC opponent played in the series.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Sunday Night Football vs. World Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sunday Night Football vs. World Series")]

On October 31, [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") aired a *[Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")* game, the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2010_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2010 Pittsburgh Steelers season") at the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2010_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2010 New Orleans Saints season"), against [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Major_League_Baseball "Fox Major League Baseball")'s coverage of the Game 4 of the [2010 World Series](/wiki/2010_World_Series "2010 World Series"), a practice the league had traditionally avoided.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The Saints won this game 20–10.

### Thanksgiving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Thanksgiving")]

See also: [NFL on Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day")

As has been the case since [2006](/wiki/2006_NFL_season "2006 NFL season"), three games were scheduled for Thursday, November 25, with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2010_New_England_Patriots_season "2010 New England Patriots season") at the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2010_Detroit_Lions_season "2010 Detroit Lions season") and the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2010_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2010 New Orleans Saints season") at the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2010_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2010 Dallas Cowboys season") in the traditional afternoon doubleheader, and the [Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/2010_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2010 Cincinnati Bengals season") at the [New York Jets](/wiki/2010_New_York_Jets_season "2010 New York Jets season") in primetime. The Patriots, Saints, and Jets won the games 45–24, 30–27, and 26–10 respectively.

### Christmas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Christmas")]

Christmas Day landed on a Saturday in 2010. The league scheduled [one game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Christmas_games "National Football League Christmas games"), the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2010_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2010 Dallas Cowboys season") at the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2010_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2010 Arizona Cardinals season") that evening, airing on [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network"). The Cardinals won the game 27–26.

### Week 17: Division games only

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Week 17: Division games only")]

The entire Week 17 schedule, played on January 2, consisted solely of divisional contests, in an attempt to increase competition after several cases over the last few seasons of playoff-bound teams resting their regular starters and playing their reserves. This has continued since then.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Scheduling changes")]

* The [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles")–[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") and [Tampa Bay](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers")–[Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") games in Week 12 were moved from 1:00 pm [EST](/wiki/North_American_Eastern_Time_Zone "North American Eastern Time Zone") to 4:15 pm EST.[[69]](#cite_note-69)
* The [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons")–Tampa Bay game in Week 13 was moved from 1:00 pm to 4:15 pm EST.[[70]](#cite_note-70)
* The [New England](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots")–Chicago game in Week 14 was moved from 1:00 pm to 4:15 pm EST[[71]](#cite_note-71)
* The Week 14 [New York Giants](/wiki/2010_New_York_Giants_season "2010 New York Giants season")-[Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") game was changed from Sunday, December 12 at 1:00 pm EST to Monday, December 13 at 8:20 pm EST because of the collapse of the [Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey_Metrodome "Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome")'s roof. (See "[Stadiums](#Stadiums)" below.)
* By way of [flexible scheduling](/wiki/NFL_on_Television#Flexible_scheduling "NFL on Television"), three game times were changed in Week 16: The Minnesota–[Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") game, originally scheduled for 1:00 pm EST on [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), was flexed into the 8:20 pm time slot on [NBC's Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"). The originally-scheduled NBC Sunday Night game between [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers")–[Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati_Bengals "Cincinnati Bengals") was changed to a 4:05 pm EST kickoff on [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS"). The [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks")–Tampa Bay game was moved from 1:00 pm to 4:15 pm EST.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
  + The Minnesota-Philadelphia game was then postponed to Tuesday, December 28 at 8 pm due to public safety concerns resulting from [an anticipated snowstorm](/wiki/December_2010_North_American_blizzard "December 2010 North American blizzard") in Philadelphia, even though no snow had fallen at the time of the postponement. The resulting game was the first Tuesday NFL game since 1946.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* By way of flexible scheduling, the following Week 17 games were changed: The St. Louis-Seattle game, originally scheduled at 4:15 pm EST, was moved onto Sunday Night Football. Also, the Jacksonville-Houston, Tennessee-Indianapolis, Chicago-Green Bay, Dallas-Philadelphia and New York Giants-Washington matches were all rescheduled from 1:00 pm to the 4:15 pm slot.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Except for Cowboys-Eagles, all these games carried playoff implications. (Per its flexible scheduling rules for Week 17, the league had to commit to move these games a full six days in advance before the aforementioned Week 16 Vikings-Eagles game eventually played out on that Tuesday night. Had Philadelphia won that game instead of Minnesota, they would have still been in contention for a first round playoff bye.[[75]](#cite_note-75))

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2010–11 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_NFL_playoffs "2010–11 NFL playoffs")

The 2010–11 NFL playoff tournament began January 8–9, 2011 with wild card weekend. Following that, the divisional playoffs set the matchups for the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game"), to be played at 3:00 pm EST on January 23, and the [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game"), to be played at 6:30 pm EST.

After a backlash from players and critics about [the previous season's Pro Bowl](/wiki/2010_Pro_Bowl "2010 Pro Bowl") being played at [Sun Life Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Life_Stadium "Sun Life Stadium") in [Miami Gardens, Florida](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida"), in the [contiguous United States](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States"), the [2011 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2011_Pro_Bowl "2011 Pro Bowl") was played at [Aloha Stadium](/wiki/Aloha_Stadium "Aloha Stadium") in [Halawa](/wiki/Halawa,_Hawaii "Halawa, Hawaii"), [Honolulu, Hawaiʻi](/wiki/Honolulu_County,_Hawaii "Honolulu County, Hawaii").[[77]](#cite_note-92)
The date was January 30, 2011, the week before the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"). An NFL spokesman stated that "Plans for future Pro Bowls are not final."[[78]](#cite_note-ESPN.comPrecedeSuperBowl-93) [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") President [Bill Polian](/wiki/Bill_Polian "Bill Polian") has stated his objections to the format, and is in favor of returning the game to after the Super Bowl as in previous years.[[79]](#cite_note-94)

The annual [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") had previously been played in Hawaii for 30 consecutive seasons from 1980 to 2009.[[80]](#cite_note-NFL.com-2011ProBowl-95) However, the NFL and State of Hawaiʻi officials only agreed to a two-year deal to hold the Pro Bowl at Aloha Stadium in 2011 and 2012.[[80]](#cite_note-NFL.com-2011ProBowl-95) This gives the option of playing the Pro Bowl in Hawaiʻi on a rotational basis with the mainland, so it both maintains the traditional ties of holding it on the islands and providing accessibility to fans when played in the contiguous 48 states.[[80]](#cite_note-NFL.com-2011ProBowl-95)

[Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV"), was held at [Cowboys Stadium](/wiki/Cowboys_Stadium "Cowboys Stadium") in Arlington, Texas, on February 6, 2011, and was the NFL's final event of the 2010 season.

Within each conference, the four division winners and the top two [non-division winners with the best overall regular season records)](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") qualified for the playoffs. The four division winners are [seeded](/wiki/Seed_(sports) "Seed (sports)") 1–4 based on their overall won-lost-tied record, and the wild card teams are seeded 5–6. The NFL does not use a fixed bracket playoff system, and there are no restrictions regarding teams from the same division matching up in any round. In the first round, dubbed the *wild-card playoffs* or *wild-card weekend*, the third-seeded division winner hosts the sixth-seed wild card, and the fourth seed hosts the fifth. The 1 and 2 seeds from each conference received a [first-round bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)"). In the second round, the *divisional playoffs*, the number 1 seed hosts the worst-surviving seed from the first round (seed 4, 5, or 6), while the number 2 seed will play the other team (seed 3, 4, or 5). The two surviving teams from each conference's divisional playoff games met in the respective AFC and NFC Conference Championship games, hosted by the higher seed. Although the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), the championship round of the playoffs, is played at a neutral site, the designated home team is based on an annual rotation by conference.[[81]](#cite_note-yahoo-96)

## Super Bowl and conference logo, trophy changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Super Bowl and conference logo, trophy changes")]

Starting with Super Bowl XLV, the template of all Super Bowl logos will virtually remain the same. The only differences from year to year will be the stadium backdrop and the Roman numerals for the game as well as colors of the area. For Super Bowl XLV, [Cowboys Stadium](/wiki/Cowboys_Stadium "Cowboys Stadium") is featured and "XLV" signifying the forty-fifth Super Bowl game.[[82]](#cite_note-Watkins-97)

The NFL also introduced new [Lamar Hunt](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt "Lamar Hunt") and [George Halas](/wiki/George_Halas "George Halas") trophies for the AFC and NFC Championship games. The trophies were changed from a brown base with an 'A' or 'N' on top of it surrounded by players layered on a [frieze](/wiki/Frieze "Frieze") upon a wall, to silver trophies in the make of a football.[[82]](#cite_note-Watkins-97) Additionally, both the [NFC](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") and [AFC](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") logos were revamped and recolored to reflect the current shield adopted two years earlier and with four stars running down the inside on both logos top to bottom from left to right instead of the six surrounding the AFC and three down the side of the NFC logo as each conference has four divisions. In addition, all event and playoff logos have undergone a complete makeover in a new logo system.[[83]](#cite_note-Lukas-98)

## Records and milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Records and milestones")]

### Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Records")]

**Passing**

* **Consecutive passes without an interception:** 335, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") ([Bernie Kosar](/wiki/Bernie_Kosar "Bernie Kosar"), 308)
* **Consecutive games with no interceptions, 2+ touchdowns:** 9, Tom Brady ([Don Meredith](/wiki/Don_Meredith "Don Meredith"), 6)

**Starts**

* **Consecutive starts by a quarterback:** 297, [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre")
* **Consecutive starts to start a career:** 208, [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") ([Gene Upshaw](/wiki/Gene_Upshaw "Gene Upshaw"))

**Turnovers**

* **Number of times sacked, career:** 525, Brett Favre ([John Elway](/wiki/John_Elway "John Elway"), 516)
* **Fumbles, career:** 166, Brett Favre ([Warren Moon](/wiki/Warren_Moon "Warren Moon"), 161)
* **Fewest turnovers by a team, season:** 10, New England Patriots[[84]](#cite_note-99)
* **Most consecutive games without a turnover**, 7, [New England Patriots](/wiki/2010_New_England_Patriots_season "2010 New England Patriots season")
* **Most touchdown, fumbles recovered, own and opponents', season**, 7, [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2010_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2010 Arizona Cardinals season") (3 own, 4 opp)
* **Most touchdown, own fumbles recovered, season**, 3, [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2010_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2010 Arizona Cardinals season")
* **Most touchdown, opponents' fumbles recovered, season**, 4, [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2010_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2010 Arizona Cardinals season")

**Special teams**

* **Kicks returned for touchdowns, career:** 14, [Devin Hester](/wiki/Devin_Hester "Devin Hester") ([Brian Mitchell](/wiki/Brian_Mitchell_(running_back) "Brian Mitchell (running back)"), 13)
* **Most touchdown, kickoff returns, team, game**, 2 (tied), [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2010_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2010 Seattle Seahawks season") (vs [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2010_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2010 San Diego Chargers season")), September 26, 2010

**Sacks**

* **Most sacks, single team, half**, 9, [New York Giants](/wiki/2010_New_York_Giants_season "2010 New York Giants season") (vs [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2010_Chicago_Bears_season "2010 Chicago Bears season"))
* **Most combined sacks (both teams), half**, 11, [New York Giants](/wiki/2010_New_York_Giants_season "2010 New York Giants season") (9) vs [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2010_Chicago_Bears_season "2010 Chicago Bears season") (2), October 3, 2010

**Playoff records**

* **Worst record to make the playoffs:** 7–9, [2010 Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2010_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2010 Seattle Seahawks season") ([1982 Cleveland Browns](/wiki/1982_Cleveland_Browns_season "1982 Cleveland Browns season") and [1982 Detroit Lions](/wiki/1982_Detroit_Lions_season "1982 Detroit Lions season"), 4–5)
* **Most playoff games won, all-time (tie):** 33, [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers")
* **Most consecutive playoff games lost:** 7, [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs")
* **Team with the worst record to win 3 postseason games on the road**, 10–6 (tied), [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2010_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2010 Green Bay Packers season")[[85]](#cite_note-100)

### All-time records set or tied

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: All-time records set or tied")]

* **Most Super Bowl appearances, all-time**, 8, [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers")
* **Most league championships, all-time**, 13, [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers")
* **Longest home game losing streak**, 14 games, [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") (2008–2010)
* **Longest road game losing streak**, 26 games, [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") (2007–2010)

### Milestones and firsts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Milestones and firsts")]

* [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") became the first quarterback to throw for 70,000 career yards.
* Brett Favre became the first quarterback to throw for 500 career touchdowns.
* Brett Favre became the first quarterback to attempt 10,000 career passes.
* Brett Favre became the second non-kicker to play in 300 games (first was [Jerry Rice](/wiki/Jerry_Rice "Jerry Rice")).
* [DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") became the first player in NFL history to win a game by scoring on a punt return as time expired.

  Further information: [Miracle at the New Meadowlands](/wiki/Miracle_at_the_New_Meadowlands "Miracle at the New Meadowlands")
* The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") became the first team since the 1970 [AFL–NFL merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") to go unbeaten in their division and miss the playoffs.
* The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") became the first team to win a division with a losing record (7–9).
* The Seattle Seahawks became the first team to win a playoff game with a losing record.
* Defensive end [Osi Umenyiora](/wiki/Osi_Umenyiora "Osi Umenyiora") set the NFL record for forced fumbles in a season, with 10.
* The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") became the first team since the [1962 Detroit Lions](/wiki/1962_Detroit_Lions_season "1962 Detroit Lions season") to never trail a single game by more than 7 points at any time.
* The [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") became the first team since the merger to start 10 rookies and still complete a winning season (10–6). However, the Buccaneers missed the playoffs.[[86]](#cite_note-101)

## Stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Stadiums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/New_Meadowlands_stadium_exterior.jpg/250px-New_Meadowlands_stadium_exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Meadowlands_stadium_exterior.jpg)

New Meadowlands Stadium

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Arrowhead_Stadium_2010.JPG/250px-Arrowhead_Stadium_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arrowhead_Stadium_2010.JPG)

Arrowhead Stadium after renovations.

[New Meadowlands Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") opened in 2010, replacing [Giants Stadium](/wiki/Giants_Stadium "Giants Stadium") as the home of both the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"). The new stadium is located a few hundred feet away from the old building in the parking lot of [Meadowlands Sports Complex](/wiki/Meadowlands_Sports_Complex "Meadowlands Sports Complex") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"). Unlike Giants Stadium (in which the Giants were the sole NFL tenant until the 1984 season), the new Meadowlands Stadium will be a 50/50 partnership between both New York teams. The Giants played their first regular season game on September 12 against the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers"), while the Jets played the following night against the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") in the first game of a *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* doubleheader.

[Arrowhead Stadium](/wiki/Arrowhead_Stadium "Arrowhead Stadium"), home of the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") since 1972, underwent a two-year $375 million renovation project which was completed and unveiled in July 2010. The stadium hosted the second game of the *Monday Night Football* opening weekend doubleheader when the Chiefs played the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers").

[M&T Bank Stadium](/wiki/M%26T_Bank_Stadium "M&T Bank Stadium"), home of the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens"), installed [FieldTurf](/wiki/FieldTurf "FieldTurf") prior to the 2010 season. The field had been Sportexe Momentum Turf since 2002 and grass before that.

Prior to Week 14, the inflatable roof of the [Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey_Metrodome "Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome"), home of the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), buckled and tore as a result of heavy snowfall in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), spilling snow onto Mall of America field and rendering the stadium unusable for the remainder of the Vikings' season.[[97]](#cite_note-112) The Vikings' scheduled home game against the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") was moved to Detroit's [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field") and postponed to a 7:20 pm EST kickoff on Monday. Though stadium workers were initially "optimistic" that the roof could be repaired before the Vikings faced the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") on December 20,[[98]](#cite_note-113) stadium officials determined that such a repair was not possible in that time frame and the game was moved to [TCF Bank Stadium](/wiki/TCF_Bank_Stadium "TCF Bank Stadium").[[99]](#cite_note-114)[[100]](#cite_note-roofta-115)

Two stadiums received new [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights"): On January 20, LandShark Stadium, the home field of the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins"), was renamed [Sun Life Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Life_Stadium "Sun Life Stadium"). The Dolphins' home field, originally named Joe Robbie Stadium from 1987 to 1996, has undergone several name changes in its history, including Pro Player Stadium (1996–2005), Dolphin Stadium (2006–2009), and most recently, LandShark Stadium.[[101]](#cite_note-116) On July 27, Jacksonville Municipal Stadium, the home field of the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars"), was renamed [EverBank Field](/wiki/EverBank_Field "EverBank Field").[[102]](#cite_note-117)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

In the 2010 season, the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") were the only team who made a major change to their main uniforms, wearing gold pants with their burgundy jerseys, and except for a game against the Packers, wore them for home games instead of their white jerseys and red pants. The white pants were not abandoned entirely, and were worn together with the burgundy jerseys for the two away games (and one home game) in which their opponent wore white at home. This was made possible with a sleeve modification, in which the broad yellow and white stripes were severely shrunken on an elastic band (same for white jerseys) so that when wearing the gold pants, the team also wore the retro style socks that had a different stripe pattern matching the sleeves of the day, so there is no longer a stripe design conflict.

The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") became the first team to officially unveil a third uniform for 2010, a [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") based on their 1929 uniforms when they won their first NFL championship. The throwbacks are as accurate as possible while complying with current NFL guidelines, with a brown modern-shell helmet in place of the leather helmets of 1929, along with blue jerseys and gold circles with the jersey numbers nested within the circles, and brown pants. Like throwbacks worn in recent seasons by the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers"), [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), and the archrival [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), these throwbacks will be a permanent addition to the Packers uniforms, unlike throwbacks worn by the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") that were intended as one-time deals but made permanent, as well as several one-shot throwbacks in recent years. The new Packers throwbacks replace the previous throwbacks (which comprised the current helmets with the "G" logo and stripes removed, white jerseys with plain green lettering, and tan pants) worn sporadically since the early 2000s (decade).

Also going the throwback route were the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears"), who harkened back to the [Sid Luckman](/wiki/Sid_Luckman "Sid Luckman") era with a 1940s set, replacing the pumpkin orange third jerseys, and the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"), who will wear [1955](/wiki/1955_NFL_season "1955 NFL season") throwbacks as well. Since the Colts only have two colors, they only have previously worn a throwback jersey once in their history, in [2004](/wiki/2004_NFL_season "2004 NFL season"). The difference between the 2004 throwback and the 2010 throwback is the helmet color, which reverses the 2004 scheme.

The [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") wore their 1999 Throwback Jerseys in Week 8 against the panthers, reminiscing on the 1995 Panthers vs Rams inaugural home game.

The [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"), who were the only team to not wear a third jersey in any form since the NFL allowed third jerseys in [2002](/wiki/2002_NFL_season "2002 NFL season"), unveiled a black third jersey to be worn in 2010.

The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") have adopted their [1960](/wiki/1960_NFL_season "1960 NFL season") championship uniforms that were worn September 12 against the Packers, the team they beat to win their last championship in celebration of the 50th anniversary of that game.

The [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") returned to using navy blue jerseys as their third jersey, after a one-year hiatus in which they wore light blue Houston Oilers throwback jerseys in celebration of the 50th anniversary of the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"), but did not wear them for any game in 2010.

The Pittsburgh Steelers wore their throwbacks against the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") on October 18 and against the New England Patriots on November 14.[[103]](#cite_note-118)

The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") have retired the neon green uniform worn for one game in [2009](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season") against Chicago, which was in turn an offshoot from an [April Fools' Day](/wiki/April_Fools%27_Day "April Fools' Day") joke written about by Uni Watch founder Paul Lukas that year.[[104]](#cite_note-119)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_NFL_season&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Media")]

For local broadcasters, see [List of current NFL broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_current_NFL_broadcasters "List of current NFL broadcasters").

This was the fifth season under the [television contracts](/wiki/NFL_on_television "NFL on television") with the league's television partners: [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") (all AFC Sunday afternoon away games and one [Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") game), [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") (all NFC Sunday afternoon away games and one Thanksgiving game), [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") (17 Sunday night games and the kickoff game), [ESPN](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") (17 Monday night games over sixteen weeks), [NFL Network](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") (eight late-season games on Thursday and Saturday nights, including one Thanksgiving game), and [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV")'s [NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket") package. These contracts ran through at least 2013.

[Joe Theismann](/wiki/Joe_Theismann "Joe Theismann") joined [Bob Papa](/wiki/Bob_Papa "Bob Papa") and [Matt Millen](/wiki/Matt_Millen "Matt Millen") in a three-man booth for [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network"), while [Keith Olbermann](/wiki/Keith_Olbermann "Keith Olbermann") left his position as co-host of NBC's *[Football Night in America](/wiki/Football_Night_in_America "Football Night in America")*.

In national radio, this is the second year on [Westwood One](/wiki/NFL_on_Westwood_One "NFL on Westwood One")'s most recent contract extension. The network also agreed to a four-year extension on December 23, 2010.

[Nielsen Ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_Ratings "Nielsen Ratings") for the fall 2010 television season have shown viewership increases of up to 10 percent for most of the NFL's broadcast partners; eighteen of the twenty most watched television broadcasts of the season have so far been NFL games.[[105]](#cite_note-120)
